title: Amazon Product Finder – A Local Python Agent
tagline: Build a tiny command‑line agent that searches Amazon and returns product titles and URLs.
description: In this tutorial you will create a small, beginner‑friendly Python project that queries Amazon’s public search page, parses the HTML, and prints a short list of matching products. You will learn how to structure a modern Python package, write a CLI with Typer, scrape safely with Requests and BeautifulSoup, and add a couple of unit tests.
problem_statement: There is no ready‑made local tool that lets a developer quickly look up Amazon products from the terminal. Existing solutions require cloud APIs or paid credentials. The goal of this tutorial is to build a lightweight, self‑contained agent that performs a simple search and returns human‑readable results.
end_state: At the end of the tutorial you will have a pip‑installable package `amazon_finder` with a `amazon-finder` console command. Running `amazon-finder "wireless headphones"` will display the first ten product titles and URLs from Amazon’s search results.
prerequisites: Familiarity with the command line (creating virtual environments, running scripts).
A recent version of Python (>=3.12) installed.
learning_outcomes: Create a modern Python project with a `pyproject.toml` file.
Write a simple CLI using Typer.
Perform HTTP GET requests with Requests and parse HTML with BeautifulSoup.
Structure code into reusable modules and add type hints.
best_practices: Pin dependency versions in `pyproject.toml` to avoid breaking changes.
Add a custom `User‑Agent` header when scraping to be polite to Amazon’s servers.
Keep network code isolated from CLI logic – makes testing easier.
Use `dataclasses` for simple data structures (e.g., a `Product`).
Write type‑annotated functions and enable `mypy` in CI (not covered here but recommended).
intro_transcript: Hey everyone! Welcome back to ProjectPalAI. Today we’re going to build a tiny but useful Python agent that can search Amazon right from your terminal. No cloud keys, no paid APIs – just a few lines of code that fetch the public search page, scrape the product titles, and print them out. By the end of this 3‑hour tutorial you’ll have a pip‑installable package called `amazon_finder` with a `amazon-finder` command you can run anywhere. Let’s dive in!

steps.title: Create the project skeleton
steps.goal: Set up a fresh Git‑style folder with `pyproject.toml`, `README.md`, and the `src/` package directory.
bullets: Create a new folder called `amazon_finder_tutorial`.
Run `python -m venv .venv` and activate it.
Create the files listed in `repo_layout`.
steps.speaker_notes: First things first – open your terminal and make a new directory for the tutorial. We'll call it `amazon_finder_tutorial`. Inside we’ll create a virtual environment so our dependencies stay isolated. Then we’ll add the `pyproject.toml` file that tells Poetry (or any PEP‑517 build backend) what we need, a short `README.md` that explains the project, and the `src/` folder where our package lives. This is the standard modern layout you’ll see in most open‑source Python projects today.

steps.title: Write `pyproject.toml`
steps.goal: Declare the build system, Python version, and runtime dependencies.
bullets: Specify `build-backend = "setuptools.build_meta"`.
Add `requests`, `beautifulsoup4`, and `typer` with version pins.
Set `python_requires = ">=3.12"`.
steps.speaker_notes: Open `pyproject.toml` and paste the minimal configuration you see on the screen. We’re using the built‑in setuptools backend because it works everywhere, and we pin each third‑party library to a known good version. Notice the `python_requires` field – that tells pip we need at least Python 3.12. Save the file and run `pip install -e .` to install the package in editable mode.

code.code:
# pyproject.toml
[build-system]
requires = ["setuptools>=68.0", "wheel"]
build-backend = "setuptools.build_meta"

[project]
name = "amazon_finder"
version = "0.1.0"
description = "A tiny CLI that searches Amazon and prints product titles."
readme = "README.md"
requires-python = ">=3.12"
license = {text = "MIT"}

[project.dependencies]
requests = ">=2.31"
beautifulsoup4 = ">=4.12"
typer = ">=0.9"

[project.scripts]
amazon-finder = "amazon_finder.main:app"

steps.title: Add a minimal package initializer
bullets: Create an empty `__init__.py` that exports the public API.
steps.speaker_notes: The `__init__.py` file can be tiny – just a docstring and an `__all__` list so that tools know what we intend to expose. This also tells Python that `src/amazon_finder` is a package, which is required for the import system to work.

code.code:
# src/amazon_finder/__init__.py
"""Amazon Finder package – provides a CLI to search Amazon products."""

__all__ = ["main", "search"]

steps.title: Create the CLI with Typer
steps.goal: Expose a `amazon-finder` command that accepts a search query.
bullets: Import `typer` and create an `app` instance.
Define a `search` command that receives a string argument.
Print a friendly welcome message.
steps.speaker_notes: Now we’ll build the command‑line interface. Typer makes this a breeze – it’s built on top of Click but adds automatic type hints and nice help messages. We’ll create an `app` object, add a single command called `search`, and forward the user’s query to a function we’ll write later. Run `amazon-finder "wireless headphones"` after we finish the next step and you’ll see a placeholder output.

steps.title: Define a simple data model
steps.goal: Create a `Product` dataclass to hold title and URL.
bullets: Use `@dataclass` from the standard library.
Add `title: str` and `url: str` fields.
steps.speaker_notes: Having a small, typed container for each product makes the rest of the code cleaner. We’ll put the dataclass in `search.py` because it belongs to the scraping layer. This also demonstrates how to use type hints and the `dataclasses` module, both of which are part of modern Python.

code.code:
# src/amazon_finder/search.py (add at top)
from __future__ import annotations

import requests
from bs4 import BeautifulSoup
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import List

@dataclass
class Product:
    """Simple container for an Amazon product result."""
    title: str
    url: str

steps.title: Implement the scraper function
steps.goal: Write `find_products(query)` that returns a list of `Product` objects.
bullets: Build the Amazon search URL (`https://www.amazon.com/s?k={query}`) with URL‑encoding.
Send a GET request with a custom `User‑Agent` header.
Parse the HTML with BeautifulSoup, locate product containers, extract title and link.
Return up to 10 products.
steps.speaker_notes: Here’s the heart of the project. We construct the search URL, add a polite `User‑Agent` so Amazon doesn’t block us immediately, and then parse the response. Amazon’s HTML is a bit noisy, but each product lives inside a `<div>` with `data-component-type="s-search-result"`. Inside that div we can find an `<h2>` with an `<a>` tag – the `href` gives the relative link, and the text gives the title. We’ll slice the results to the first ten items to keep the output short. Remember, this is for learning – for production you’d want a proper API key or a more robust scraper.

code.code:
# src/amazon_finder/search.py (continue)
import urllib.parse

HEADERS = {
    "User-Agent": (
        "Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; Win64; x64) "
        "AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) "
        "Chrome/124.0.0.0 Safari/537.36"
    )
}

def _build_search_url(query: str) -> str:
    encoded = urllib.parse.quote_plus(query)
    return f"https://www.amazon.com/s?k={encoded}"

def find_products(query: str, max_results: int = 10) -> List[Product]:
    """Fetch Amazon search results and return a list of :class:`Product`.

    Parameters
    ----------
    query: str
        The search term entered by the user.
    max_results: int, optional
        How many products to return (default 10).
    """
    url = _build_search_url(query)
    response = requests.get(url, headers=HEADERS, timeout=10)
    response.raise_for_status()
    soup = BeautifulSoup(response.text, "html.parser")
    results: List[Product] = []
    for div in soup.select('div[data-component-type="s-search-result"]'):
        # Title
        title_tag = div.select_one('h2 a span')
        if not title_tag:
            continue
        title = title_tag.get_text(strip=True)
        # URL – Amazon uses relative links
        link_tag = div.select_one('h2 a')
        if not link_tag or not link_tag.get('href'):
            continue
        rel_url = link_tag['href']
        full_url = urllib.parse.urljoin('https://www.amazon.com', rel_url)
        results.append(Product(title=title, url=full_url))
        if len(results) >= max_results:
            break
    return results

steps.title: Write the first unit test
steps.goal: Test that `find_products` correctly parses a saved HTML fixture.
bullets: Create a `tests/fixtures/sample.html` file with a tiny snippet of Amazon search HTML.
Load the fixture in the test, monkeypatch `requests.get` to return it.
Assert that the returned list contains the expected titles and URLs.
steps.speaker_notes: Testing web‑scraping code can be flaky if you hit the live site, so we’ll use a static HTML fixture. Pytest’s `monkeypatch` fixture lets us replace `requests.get` with a fake function that returns our sample HTML. This way the test runs instantly and deterministically. We’ll place the fixture in a `tests/fixtures` folder – keep it tiny, just enough to cover the selectors we wrote earlier.

code.code:
# tests/test_search.py
import pathlib
from unittest.mock import Mock

import pytest
from amazon_finder.search import find_products, Product

FIXTURE_PATH = pathlib.Path(__file__).parent / "fixtures" / "sample.html"

def _fake_get(url, headers=None, timeout=None):
    # Return an object that mimics `requests.Response`
    mock_resp = Mock()
    mock_resp.status_code = 200
    mock_resp.text = FIXTURE_PATH.read_text(encoding="utf-8")
    mock_resp.raise_for_status = Mock()
    return mock_resp

def test_find_products_parses_fixture(monkeypatch):
    monkeypatch.setattr("requests.get", _fake_get)
    products = find_products("dummy")
    assert len(products) == 2
    assert products[0].title == "Echo Dot (5th Gen) | Smart speaker with Alexa"
    assert products[0].url.startswith("https://www.amazon.com/dp/B09B8")
    assert isinstance(products[1], Product)

steps.title: Add the HTML fixture file
steps.goal: Provide a minimal but realistic snippet of Amazon search results for the test.
bullets: Create `tests/fixtures/sample.html` with two product entries.
Make sure the markup matches the selectors used in `search.py`.
steps.speaker_notes: Here’s the tiny HTML we’ll use. It contains two `<div>` elements with the `data-component-type="s-search-result"` attribute, each holding an `<h2>` with a link and a `<span>` for the title. This mirrors the real Amazon page closely enough for our selectors to work, but it’s tiny so the test runs instantly.

code.code:
# tests/fixtures/sample.html

Echo Dot (5th Gen) | Smart speaker with Alexa

Fire TV Stick 4K with Alexa Voice Remote

steps.title: Run the test suite and fix bugs
steps.goal: Execute `pytest` and ensure the scraper passes the fixture test.
bullets: Run `pytest -q`.
If the test fails, check selector strings and URL joining logic.
Add a small `except Exception` block in `find_products` to surface errors nicely.
steps.speaker_notes: Now let’s run the tests. In the terminal type `pytest -q`. You should see one passing test. If something goes wrong – maybe the CSS selector changed – adjust the selector in `search.py`. Also we’ll wrap the whole request/parse block in a try‑except that re‑raises a `RuntimeError` with a helpful message; this keeps the CLI clean and gives users a clue what went wrong.

code.code:
# src/amazon_finder/search.py (add at top of find_products)
    try:
        url = _build_search_url(query)
        response = requests.get(url, headers=HEADERS, timeout=10)
        response.raise_for_status()
        soup = BeautifulSoup(response.text, "html.parser")
        # ... existing parsing logic ...
    except Exception as exc:
        raise RuntimeError(f"Failed to fetch Amazon results: {exc}") from exc

steps.title: Final polish and run the CLI
steps.goal: Install the package, try a real search, and wrap up.
bullets: Run `pip install -e .` again to pick up any changes.
Execute `amazon-finder "mechanical keyboard"`.
Observe the nicely formatted list of product titles and URLs.
Update the README with usage instructions.
steps.speaker_notes: Everything is wired up! Install the package in editable mode, then run the command we defined in `pyproject.toml`. You should see a short list of the first ten products that match your query. If you get a CAPTCHA or a block from Amazon, just know that this is a simple demo – for heavy usage you’d need to respect robots.txt or use the official Product Advertising API. Finally, we’ll add a quick usage section to the README so future visitors know how to run the tool.

outro_transcript: Congratulations! You just built a fully functional, pip‑installable Python agent that searches Amazon from the command line. We covered project scaffolding, a clean CLI with Typer, safe HTTP requests, HTML parsing with BeautifulSoup, a tiny data model, and unit testing with pytest. Feel free to extend the project – maybe add price extraction, pagination, or switch to the official Amazon PA‑API for production use. Thanks for watching, and happy coding!
final_project_code:
# pyproject.toml [build-system] requires = ["setuptools>=68.0", "wheel"] build-backend = "setuptools.build_meta" [project] name = "amazon_finder" version = "0.1.0" description = "A tiny CLI that searches Amazon and prints product titles." readme = "README.md" requires-python = ">=3.12" license = {text = "MIT"} [project.dependencies] requests = ">=2.31" beautifulsoup4 = ">=4.12" typer = ">=0.9" [project.scripts] amazon-finder = "amazon_finder.main:app" # --- README.md --- # Amazon Product Finder A small Python CLI that searches Amazon and prints the first few product titles and URLs. ## Installation ```bash python -m venv .venv source .venv/bin/activate pip install -e . ``` ## Usage ```bash amazon-finder "wireless headphones" ``` ## Development ```bash pip install -e .[dev] pytest -q ``` --- # src/amazon_finder/__init__.py """Amazon Finder package – provides a CLI to search Amazon products.""" __all__ = ["main", "search"] --- # src/amazon_finder/main.py import typer from .search import find_products app = typer.Typer(help="Search Amazon from the terminal.") @app.command() def search(query: str = typer.Argument(..., help="What you want to find on Amazon")): """CLI entry point – forwards the query to the scraper and prints results.""" typer.echo(f"🔎 Searching Amazon for: {query}\n") products = find_products(query) if not products: typer.echo("❌ No products found. Try a different query.") raise typer.Exit(code=1) for idx, prod in enumerate(products, start=1): typer.echo(f"{idx}. {prod.title}\n {prod.url}\n") if __name__ == "__main__": app() --- # src/amazon_finder/search.py from __future__ import annotations import urllib.parse from dataclasses import dataclass from typing import List import requests from bs4 import BeautifulSoup HEADERS = { "User-Agent": ( "Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; Win64; x64) " "AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) " "Chrome/124.0.0.0 Safari/537.36" ) } @dataclass class Product: """Simple container for an Amazon product result.""" title: str url: str def _build_search_url(query: str) -> str: encoded = urllib.parse.quote_plus(query) return f"https://www.amazon.com/s?k={encoded}" def find_products(query: str, max_results: int = 10) -> List[Product]: """Fetch Amazon search results and return a list of :class:`Product`. Parameters ---------- query: str The search term entered by the user. max_results: int, optional How many products to return (default 10). """ try: url = _build_search_url(query) response = requests.get(url, headers=HEADERS, timeout=10) response.raise_for_status() soup = BeautifulSoup(response.text, "html.parser") results: List[Product] = [] for div in soup.select('div[data-component-type="s-search-result"]'): title_tag = div.select_one('h2 a span') if not title_tag: continue title = title_tag.get_text(strip=True) link_tag = div.select_one('h2 a') if not link_tag or not link_tag.get('href'): continue rel_url = link_tag['href'] full_url = urllib.parse.urljoin('https://www.amazon.com', rel_url) results.append(Product(title=title, url=full_url)) if len(results) >= max_results: break return results except Exception as exc: raise RuntimeError(f"Failed to fetch Amazon results: {exc}") from exc --- # tests/test_search.py import pathlib from unittest.mock import Mock import pytest from amazon_finder.search import find_products, Product FIXTURE_PATH = pathlib.Path(__file__).parent / "fixtures" / "sample.html" def _fake_get(url, headers=None, timeout=None): mock_resp = Mock() mock_resp.status_code = 200 mock_resp.text = FIXTURE_PATH.read_text(encoding="utf-8") mock_resp.raise_for_status = Mock() return mock_resp def test_find_products_parses_fixture(monkeypatch): monkeypatch.setattr("requests.get", _fake_get) products = find_products("dummy") assert len(products) == 2 assert products[0].title == "Echo Dot (5th Gen) | Smart speaker with Alexa" assert products[0].url.startswith("https://www.amazon.com/dp/B09B8") assert isinstance(products[1], Product) --- # tests/fixtures/sample.html

Echo Dot (5th Gen) | Smart speaker with Alexa

Fire TV Stick 4K with Alexa Voice Remote
how_to_run:
1. Clone the repository or copy the generated files into a folder.
2. Open a terminal, navigate to the project root, and create a virtual environment:
   ```bash
   python -m venv .venv
   source .venv/bin/activate   # on Windows use `.venv\Scripts\activate`
   ```
3. Install the package in editable mode:
   ```bash
   pip install -e .
   ```
4. Run the test suite to verify everything works:
   ```bash
   pytest -q
   ```
5. Use the CLI to search Amazon:
   ```bash
   amazon-finder "wireless headphones"
   ```
   The command will print the first ten product titles and URLs.

challenges.title: Add price extraction
challenges.description: Extend the scraper to also capture the product's price (if displayed) and show it in the CLI output.
challenges.build_process:
1. Inspect the HTML of a real Amazon search result to find the CSS selector that contains the price (e.g., `span.a-price-whole`).
2. Update `Product` dataclass to include an optional `price: str` field.
3. Modify `find_products` to extract the price for each product, handling missing prices gracefully.
4. Update the CLI loop in `main.py` to display the price next to the title.
5. Add a new test case with a fixture that includes price markup and assert the price is parsed correctly.
challenges.solution_code:
# src/amazon_finder/search.py (price addition)
@dataclass
class Product:
    title: str
    url: str
    price: str | None = None

# inside find_products loop, after extracting title and url:
price_tag = div.select_one('span.a-price-whole')
price = price_tag.get_text(strip=True) if price_tag else None
results.append(Product(title=title, url=full_url, price=price))

# src/amazon_finder/main.py (display price)
for idx, prod in enumerate(products, start=1):
    price_part = f" – ${prod.price}" if prod.price else ""
    typer.echo(f"{idx}. {prod.title}{price_part}\n   {prod.url}\n")
challenges.solution_walkthrough: First we added a `price` attribute to the `Product` dataclass, defaulting to `None` for items without a price. In the scraper we look for the `` element, which Amazon uses for the integer part of the price. If the selector returns a tag we strip its text; otherwise we keep `price` as `None`. When building the list we pass the price along with title and URL. Finally, the CLI prints the price only when it exists, using a small conditional string. The new test mirrors the HTML structure with a `$99` element and asserts that `product.price == "99"`.
